last slides, I guess. So I'll just very quickly mention what I was going to mention. So another thing
you can do is create new data. One way you can create new data is just ask people to annotate data
for you. But the problem is for low resource languages, it's often hard to get lots of data. So one
thing we do is leverage something called active learning. The basic idea behind active learning
is that you use labeled data. You do some training, you get a model, then you apply that model to
lots of unlabeled data and select some data that the model is highly uncertain about and then throw
that to annotation. And so basically what this does is this allows you to select data where the
current model is not very confident or not very good or something like this. This can be really
helpful. It's not limited to multilingual learning, but it's specifically quite helpful in cases
where you can annotate only a small amount of data. And so the basic idea is illustrated here
for binary classification, where if you only annotate data randomly, you might end up getting
data that doesn't tell you very well about the specific decision boundary. And as a result,
the model trained on the few data points that you randomly select could be inaccurate. Whereas active
learning finds data directly on the decision boundary here, and that allows you to find more
effective samples. There's two fundamental ideas, uncertainty and representativeness. And you
want to come up with models, you want to come up with a data that selects data where the model is
uncertain, but representative. And so actually you can select data only for representativeness,
and it's only and it's relatively useful. So you can select only data that has lots of high
frequency phrases, for example, for machine translation. And that will allow you to get better
coverage of high frequency phrases. But uncertainty is also good because it helps you find like the
model's current blind spots. The problem with only uncertainty is it gives you lots of garbage.
It'll get like for example, for machine translation, it will select things with only emojis,
or something like that. And that's not very useful to train your model.
